items.title: Mechanisms Underlying Osteomyelitis
items.content: Osteomyelitis can arise from three primary mechanisms: hematogenous spread, contiguous spread following surgery or trauma, and secondary infection due to vascular insufficiency or neuropathy. Hematogenous osteomyelitis in adults typically involves the vertebral column, with about half of patients having a detectable primary focus such as urinary tract infections or intravascular catheterization sites.
keyPoints: Three mechanisms: hematogenous spread, contiguous spread, secondary infection due to vascular insufficiency/neuropathy
Hematogenous osteomyelitis in adults typically involves the vertebral column
About half of patients with hematogenous osteomyelitis have a detectable primary focus (e.g., urinary tract, skin/soft tissue, intravascular catheterization sites, endocardium)
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · Osteomyelitis | CLASSIFICATION

items.title: Classification by Duration and Management Implications
items.content: The classification of osteomyelitis based on the duration of infection is crucial for management. Acute osteomyelitis without bone necrosis can be treated with antibiotics alone, whereas chronic osteomyelitis requires both antibiotic therapy and debridement surgery due to the presence of bone necrosis.
keyPoints: Classification by duration: acute vs. chronic
Acute osteomyelitis without bone necrosis is treatable with antibiotics alone
Chronic osteomyelitis involves bone necrosis, necessitating both antibiotic therapy and debridement surgery
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · Osteomyelitis | CLASSIFICATION

items.title: Classification by Location and Clinical Relevance
items.content: Osteomyelitis can be classified by location into long bones, vertebral column, and periarticular bones. Long bones are typically involved in children through hematogenous seeding or after trauma/surgery. Vertebral osteomyelitis risk increases with age in adults, while periarticular osteomyelitis often complicates inadequately treated septic arthritis.
keyPoints: Classification by location: long bones, vertebral column, periarticular bones
Long bone involvement typically occurs after hematogenous seeding or trauma/surgery
Vertebral osteomyelitis risk increases with age in adults
Periarticular osteomyelitis often complicates inadequately treated septic arthritis
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · Osteomyelitis | CLASSIFICATION

items.title: Mechanisms of Vertebral Osteomyelitis Infection
items.content: Vertebral osteomyelitis is primarily a hematogenous infection in adults, involving the vertebrae and intervertebral disks. Microorganisms invade through segmental arterial circulation into avascular disks or via retrograde seeding from prevertebral venous plexus. Direct inoculation can occur during spinal surgery, epidural infiltration, or trauma.
keyPoints: Vertebral osteomyelitis is the most common form of hematogenous bone infection in adults.
Microorganisms invade avascular disks via segmental arterial circulation or retrograde seeding through prevertebral venous plexus.
Direct inoculation routes include spinal surgery, epidural infiltration, and trauma.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY

items.title: Epidemiology and Microbiology of Vertebral Osteomyelitis
items.content: Vertebral osteomyelitis is more common in males (1.5:1 ratio) with incidence increasing from 2.2 to 5.8 cases/100,000 person-years between 1995-2008. The condition is age-dependent, especially in men ≥70 years who have a sixfold higher incidence rate than those <70 years. It's typically monomicrobial; Staphylococcus aureus causes 40–50% of acute cases.
keyPoints: Incidence increased from 2.2 to 5.8 cases/100,000 person-years (1995-2008).
Higher incidence in males with a ratio of 1.5:1.
Men ≥70 years have sixfold higher incidence than those <70 years.
Staphylococcus aureus causes 40–50% of acute cases.
Subacute/chronic cases vary by region; Mycobacterium tuberculosis and Brucella species are common in endemic areas.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY

items.title: Key Symptoms and Initial Diagnostic Steps for Vertebral Osteomyelitis
items.content: Vertebral osteomyelitis presents with nonspecific symptoms, but back pain is the leading initial symptom in over 85% of cases. Fever occurs in about half of patients, often masked by analgesic use. Neurologic deficits are seen in one-third due to spinal epidural abscess. Diagnosis involves evaluating leukocytosis and neutrophilia (low sensitivity) and using ESR or CRP levels for higher diagnostic accuracy.
keyPoints: Back pain is the leading initial symptom (>85% of cases).
Fever >38°C occurs in ~50% of patients.
Neurologic deficits observed in ~33% of cases due to spinal epidural abscess.
Leukocytosis and neutrophilia have low diagnostic sensitivity (65% and 40%, respectively).
Increased ESR or CRP levels are reported in 98% and 100% of cases, respectively.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Differential Diagnosis Considerations for Febrile Back Pain
items.content: The differential diagnosis for febrile back pain is broad due to nonspecific symptoms of osteomyelitis. It includes infectious causes like pyelonephritis and pancreatitis, as well as noninfectious conditions such as osteoporotic fractures and seronegative spondylitis.
keyPoints: Differential diagnosis for febrile back pain is broad.
Includes infections (pyelonephritis, pancreatitis) and viral syndromes.
Noninfectious pathologies include osteoporotic fracture and seronegative spondylitis.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Imaging Techniques for Diagnosing Vertebral Osteomyelitis
items.content: MRI is the gold standard for diagnosing vertebral osteomyelitis, especially in patients with neurologic symptoms. CT can guide biopsies and PET with $^{18}$F-fluorodeoxyglucose offers high diagnostic accuracy when MRI is contraindicated.
keyPoints: MRI is the gold standard for diagnosis, particularly with neurologic impairment.
CT less sensitive than MRI but useful in guiding percutaneous biopsy.
PET with $^{18}$F-fluorodeoxyglucose has high diagnostic accuracy as an alternative to MRI.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Optimal Antibiotic Therapy Timing
items.content: In patients without sepsis syndrome, antibiotics should not be administered until the pathogen is identified through blood culture, bone biopsy, or aspirated pus collection.
keyPoints: Antibiotics should be withheld until identification of the infecting agent in non-septic patients.
Identification methods include blood culture, bone biopsy, or aspirated pus.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · Vertebral Osteomyelitis

items.title: Duration and Efficacy of Antibiotic Therapy
items.content: A 6-week course of antibiotics is noninferior to a 12-week course for pyogenic vertebral osteomylitis, with cure rates of 90.9% in both groups after one year.
keyPoints: Controlled trial shows 6 weeks of antibiotic treatment is as effective as 12 weeks for pyogenic vertebral osteomyelitis.
Cure rate was 90.9% in both the 6-week and 12-week therapy groups after one year.
Prolonged therapy is necessary only for undrained abscesses or patients with spinal implants.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · Vertebral Osteomyelitis

items.subtopic: COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | OSTEOMYELITIS IN LONG BONES | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Recognizing Complications of Osteomyelitis: When to Suspect and Act
items.content: Complications in osteomyelitis should be suspected with persistent pain, increased CRP levels, or new-onset/persistent neurologic impairment. Epidural abscesses are a significant concern, occurring in 15–20% of cases, more frequently in the cervical spine (30%) than lumbar spine (12%). Persistent pain despite normalized CRP suggests mechanical issues like severe osteonecrosis or spinal instability, necessitating orthopedic consultation.
keyPoints: Persistent pain with increased CRP and neurologic impairment indicates complications.
Epidural abscesses occur in 15–20% of cases; cervical spine involvement is higher (30%) than lumbar (12%).
Risk factors for severe neurologic deficit include epidural abscess, cervical/thoracic involvement, and S. aureus vertebral osteomyelitis.
Persistent pain with normalized CRP suggests mechanical complications like severe osteonecrosis or spinal instability; consult orthopedic surgeon.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | OSTEOMYELITIS IN LONG BONES | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | OSTEOMYELITIS IN LONG BONES | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.title: Global and Regional Considerations in Diagnosing Vertebral Osteomyelitis
items.content: The incidence of acute vertebral osteomyelitis is globally consistent, but subacute/chronic forms are region-specific. Brucellosis-related cases are prevalent in the Middle East, Africa, Central/South America, and India. Tuberculosis is a major cause in Africa and Asia, where over two-thirds of global TB burden exists. Patients from or traveling to these regions should undergo specific diagnostic tests.
keyPoints: Acute vertebral osteomyelitis incidence is similar worldwide; subacute/chronic forms are region-specific.
Brucellosis-related cases predominate in the Middle East, Africa, Central/South America, and India.
Tuberculosis is a frequent cause of vertebral osteomyelitis in Africa and Asia (India, Indonesia, China).
Specific diagnostic tests are needed for patients from or traveling to endemic regions.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | OSTEOMYELITIS IN LONG BONES | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Etiology of Osteomyelitis: Key Pathogens and Risk Factors
items.content: Hematogenous long-bone osteomyelitis shares a similar microbial spectrum with vertebral osteomyelitis, predominantly caused by S. aureus. In cases involving patients from endemic regions or those with impaired cellular immunity (e.g., HIV infection, post-transplantation), mycobacteria and fungi like Cryptococcus species may be involved. Coagulase-negative staphylococci are the second most common pathogens in implant-associated osteomyelitis, while gram-negative bacilli or polymicrobial organisms typically cause contiguous long-bone osteomyelitis following open fractures.
keyPoints: S. aureus is the most common pathogen in both hematogenous and vertebral osteomyelitis.
Mycobacteria and fungi (e.g., Cryptococcus, Sporothrix schenckii) are more prevalent in patients from endemic regions or with impaired immunity.
Coagulase-negative staphylococci are the second most common pathogens in implant-associated osteomyelitis.
Gram-negative bacilli or polymicrobial organisms typically cause contiguous long-bone osteomyelitis after open fractures.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · MICROBIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of Osteomyelitis: Recognizing Patterns
items.content: Primary or recurrent hematogenous long-bone osteomyelitis in adults presents with pain and low-grade fever, occasionally progressing to clinical sepsis. Post-implantation infections are categorized as early (acute), delayed, or late (chronic) based on timing, with early/acute cases showing surgical site infection signs like erythema and impaired wound healing. Delayed or chronic infections often result from low-virulence organisms or inadequate treatment of early infections, presenting with persistent pain and intermittent discharge. Brodie's abscess, a form of subacute osteomyelitis, is marked by pain (98%) and swelling (53%), primarily in the tibia or femur, along with fever and elevated inflammatory markers. Diagnosis can be delayed, with a median time from symptom onset to diagnosis being 3 months.
keyPoints: Primary symptoms of hematogenous osteomyelitis include pain and low-grade fever.
Early post-implantation infections manifest as surgical site infection signs within 3 weeks.
Delayed or chronic infections present with persistent pain, intermittent discharge, and fluctuating erythema.
Brodie's abscess is characterized by pain (98%) and swelling (53%), primarily in the tibia or femur.
Median delay from symptom onset to diagnosis of osteomyelitis is 3 months.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · MICROBIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: When to Use Imaging for Diagnosing Acute Hematogenous Long-Bone Osteomyelitis
items.content: The diagnostic approach for acute hematogenous long-bone osteomyelitis involves similar steps as vertebral osteomyelitis. A three-phase bone scan is not useful within one year post-surgery due to increased marker uptake from bone remodeling, but it aids in diagnosing late recurrences at low cost. If the bone scan results are positive, CT imaging is necessary to assess the extent of inflammation and detect necrosis.
keyPoints: Three-phase bone scan not useful within 1 year post-surgery due to increased marker uptake
Useful for rapid diagnosis in late recurrences at low cost
Positive bone scan results necessitate CT imaging to evaluate inflamed tissue extent and detect sequestra
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · DIAGNOSIS | Osteomyelitis in Long Bones

items.title: Role of SPECT/CT in Chronic Osteomyelitis Diagnosis
items.content: For osteomyelitis lasting more than one year, single-photon emission CT combined with conventional CT (SPECT/CT) is recommended. This can be performed using $^{99m}$Tc methylene diphosphonate-labeled leukocytes or labeled monoclonal antibodies to granulocytes. Surgical debridement is necessary for both diagnostic and therapeutic purposes in chronic cases.
keyPoints: SPECT/CT with $^{99m}$Tc-MDP-labeled leukocytes or labeled monoclonal antibodies recommended for osteomyelitis >1 year
Surgical debridement required for diagnosis (biopsy culture, histology) and treatment
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · DIAGNOSIS | Osteomyelitis in Long Bones

items.subtopic: COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY
items.title: Chronic Osteomyelitis and Its Management
items.content: Long-bone osteomyelitis can progress to chronic osteomyelitis, especially after internal fixation of open fractures or implant-associated infections without surgical debridement. Chronic cases often result in recurrent sinus tracts causing severe skin and soft tissue damage.
keyPoints: Chronic osteomyelitis is a main complication of long-bone osteomyelitis.
High risk post-internal fixation of open fractures or untreated implant-associated infections.
Recurrent sinus tracts cause significant skin and soft tissue damage in chronic cases.
Management requires combined orthopedic repair and plastic reconstructive surgery.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY

items.subtopic: COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY
items.title: Epidemiology of Periprosthetic Joint Infection (PJI)
items.content: The risk of PJI varies by joint type, with hip and knee arthroplasty having the lowest risk (0.3–1.5%) and ankle and elbow replacements the highest (4–10%). Hematogenous PJI is most common early postoperatively but can occur throughout life.
keyPoints: Risk of infection varies by joint: 0.3–1.5% for hip/knee, 4–10% for ankle/elbow arthroplasty.
Hematogenous PJI risk peaks early postoperatively but can develop >2 years after implantation.
Secondary PJI risk during S. aureus bacteremia is 30–40%.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY

items.title: Classification of Periprosthetic Joint Infection (PJI) for Therapeutic Decision-Making
items.content: Classifying PJI based on the timing and nature of symptoms aids in therapeutic decision-making. Acute hematogenous PJIs present with new-onset pain, often caused by S. aureus, while chronic PJIs are characterized by nonspecific symptoms like joint effusion and implant loosening due to low-virulence organisms.
keyPoints: PJI classified as acute hematogenous (<3 weeks), early postinterventional (within 1 month), or chronic (>3 weeks).
Acute hematogenous PJI often caused by S. aureus, characterized by new-onset pain.
Chronic PJI typically involves low-virulence organisms like coagulase-negative staphylococci or C. acnes, presenting with nonspecific symptoms.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · CLASSIFICATION AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Diagnostic Thresholds and Methods for PJI
items.content: Blood tests such as CRP (≥10 mg/L) and ESR (≥30 mm/h) are sensitive but not specific. Synovial fluid analysis is crucial, with leukocyte thresholds of 1700/μL for knee infections and 4200/μL for hip infections. α-defensin in synovial fluid confirms PJI despite its cost and limited sensitivity.
keyPoints: CRP levels ≥10 mg/L and ESR rates ≥30 mm/h are sensitive but not specific for PJI.
Synovial fluid leukocyte thresholds: 1700/μL for knee, 4200/μL for hip infections.
α-defensin is highly specific for confirming PJI but expensive and limited in sensitivity.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · CLASSIFICATION AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Periprosthetic Joint Infection | PREVENTION OF HEMATOGENOUS INFECTION
items.title: Antibiotic Prophylaxis for Dental Procedures in Patients with Prosthetic Joints
items.content: Patients with prosthetic joints do not require antibiotic prophylaxis during dental procedures to prevent periprosthetic joint infection.
keyPoints: The risk of prosthetic hip or knee infection is not increased following dental procedures.
Antibiotic prophylaxis is not needed during dental work for patients with prosthetic joints.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · Periprosthetic Joint Infection | PREVENTION OF HEMATOGENOUS INFECTION

items.subtopic: Periprosthetic Joint Infection | PREVENTION OF HEMATOGENOUS INFECTION
items.title: Management of Documented Bacterial Infections in Patients with Prosthetic Joints
items.content: Prompt treatment of documented bacterial infections is crucial to prevent hematogenous seeding and subsequent periprosthetic joint infection.
keyPoints: Hematogenous seeding risk is highest during S. aureus bacteremia from a distant focus.
Documented bacterial infections should be promptly treated in patients with prosthetic joints.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · Periprosthetic Joint Infection | PREVENTION OF HEMATOGENOUS INFECTION

items.title: Antimicrobial Resistance Impact on Sternal Osteomyelitis Treatment
items.content: In regions with high rifampin resistance in staphylococci and/or fluoroquinolone resistance in gram-negative bacilli, the efficacy of debridement with implant retention for treating sternal osteomyelitis is significantly reduced.
keyPoints: Rifampin and fluoroquinolones are key antimicrobials against staphylococcal and gram-negative biofilms respectively.
High resistance rates to these drugs reduce cure rates in debridement with implant retention strategies.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY

items.title: Epidemiology and Risk Factors of Sternal Osteomyelitis
items.content: Poststernotomy wound infection incidence ranges from 0.5% to 2%, increasing with risk factors like diabetes, obesity, chronic renal failure, emergency surgery, use of bilateral internal mammary artery grafts, and re-exploration for bleeding. Primary sternal osteomyelitis accounts for only 0.3% of all osteomyelitis cases.
keyPoints: Poststernotomy wound infection incidence: 0.5% to 2%
Primary sternal osteomyelitis constitutes 0.3% of all osteomyelitis cases.
Risk factors include diabetes, obesity, chronic renal failure, emergency surgery, use of bilateral internal mammary artery grafts, and re-exploration for bleeding.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY

items.title: Diagnostic Approach for Sternal Osteomyelitis in Tuberculosis-Endemic Regions
items.content: In patients with sternal osteomyelitis who have grown up in tuberculosis-endemic regions, a specific workup for mycobacterial infection is crucial, especially if the onset followed blunt sternal trauma.
keyPoints: Specific workup for mycobacterial infection should be performed in endemic areas.
Consider mycobacterial etiology post-blunt sternal trauma.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Sternal Osteomyelitis

items.title: Management of Staphylococcal Deep Sternal-Wound Infection
items.content: Immediate antimicrobial therapy is essential after obtaining samples for microbiologic analyses in deep sternal-wound infections. Initial treatment should target staphylococci, with adjustments based on local susceptibility patterns and confirmed pathogen identity.
keyPoints: Start antimicrobial therapy immediately post-sample collection.
Initial treatment targets staphylococci; consider local resistance patterns.
Use vancomycin or daptomycin in high MRSA prevalence areas.
Optimize treatment based on culture results.
Rifampin-containing regimen is associated with success.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Sternal Osteomyelitis

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ■ PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | RISK FACTORS | MICROBIOLOGY
items.title: Mortality and Risk Factors in Osteomyelitis
items.content: Primary sternal osteomyelitis has a minimal mortality risk, while secondary sternal osteomyelitis carries higher mortality rates (6–20%) within the first year post-diagnosis. Diabetic foot ulcers are significant precursors to osteomyelitis, with 34% of diabetic patients developing a foot ulcer in their lifetime and an annual progression rate to osteomyelitis of 5%. Risk factors for diabetic foot infections include neuropathy, neuro-osteoarthropathic deformities, arterial insufficiency, uncontrolled hyperglycemia, disabilities like reduced vision, and maladaptive behavior.
keyPoints: Primary sternal osteomyelitis has minimal mortality risk.
Secondary sternal osteomyelitis has a 6–20% mortality rate in the first year post-diagnosis.
34% of diabetic patients develop foot ulcers over their lifetime.
Annual progression to osteomyelitis from foot ulcer is 5%.

Risk factors for diabetic foot infection include neuropathy, neuro-osteoarthropathic deformities, arterial insufficiency, uncontrolled hyperglycemia, disabilities like reduced vision, and maladaptive behavior.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ■ PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | RISK FACTORS | MICROBIOLOGY

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ■ PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | RISK FACTORS | MICROBIOLOGY
items.title: Microbiology of Osteomyelitis and Diagnostic Challenges
items.content: In endemic areas, consider M. tuberculosis, Salmonella species, and Brucella species for osteomyelitis diagnosis. Foot osteomyelitis is primarily acquired exogenously through deep pressure ulcers or post-surgical infections. The correlation between cultures from bone biopsy versus wound swabs or soft-tissue punctures is poor (24% with needle biopsy). S. aureus shows better isolation rates (40–50%) compared to anaerobes, gram-negative bacilli, and coagulase-negative staphylococci. Leading pathogens in untreated cases include S. aureus (25–40%), anaerobes (5–20%), and various gram-negative bacilli (18–40%).
keyPoints: Consider M. tuberculosis, Salmonella species, and Brucella species in endemic areas for osteomyelitis.
Foot osteomyelitis is primarily acquired exogenously.
Correlation between bone biopsy and wound swab cultures is poor (24% with needle biopsy).
S. aureus isolation rates are 40–50%, better than anaerobes, gram-negative bacilli, or coagulase-negative staphylococci.
Leading pathogens in untreated osteomyelitis: S. aureus (25–40%), anaerobes (5–20%), and gram-negative bacilli (18–40%).
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ■ PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | RISK FACTORS | MICROBIOLOGY

items.title: Probe-to-Bone Test for High-Pretest Probability Patients
items.content: In patients with a high pretest probability of foot osteomyelitis, such as those hospitalized for chronic deep foot ulcers due to diabetes, the probe-to-bone test can be used effectively. This clinical method has a positive predictive value of approximately 90% and involves directly touching bone with a metal instrument.
keyPoints: Probe-to-bone test positive predictive value ~90%
Highly effective in patients with high pretest probability (e.g., diabetic with chronic foot ulcer)
Direct contact with bone via metal instrument confirms diagnosis
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · DIAGNOSIS | Foot Osteomyelitis

items.title: MRI for Lower Pretest Probability Patients
items.content: For patients with a lower pretest probability of foot osteomyelitis, MRI is recommended due to its high sensitivity (80–100%) and specificity (80–90%). This imaging modality should be used when clinical diagnosis via probe-to-bone test is not feasible.
keyPoints: MRI sensitivity 80–100%
MRI specificity 80–90%
Preferred for patients with lower pretest probability
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · DIAGNOSIS | Foot Osteomyelitis

items.explanation: The Cierny-Mader staging system is useful mainly for trauma surgeons.

items.question: What should be suspected if CRP values do not return to normal or rise after an initial decrease in osteomyelitis?
items.explanation: Implant-associated infection should be suspected if CRP values do not return to the normal range or rise after an initial decrease.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · DIAGNOSIS | Osteomyelitis in Long Bones

items.question: What is required following a positive result from imaging studies for diagnosing osteomyelitis?
options: CT scan to estimate the extent of inflamed tissue and detect bone necrosis
items.explanation: If imaging results are positive, CT is required to estimate the extent of inflamed tissue and detect bone necrosis.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · DIAGNOSIS | Osteomyelitis in Long Bones

items.subtopic: COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY
items.question: Which factor significantly increases the risk of progression to chronic osteomyelitis?
options: Internal fixation of an open fracture without surgical debridement
Use of antibiotics in early stages
items.explanation: The risk is especially high after internal fixation of an open fracture and among patients with implant-associated osteomyelitis that is treated without surgical debridement.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY

items.subtopic: COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY
items.question: In which population is tuberculous osteomyelitis most commonly considered in high-prevalence countries?
options: Very old people, HIV-infected patients, and immigrants from endemic countries
Individuals undergoing hip and knee arthroplasty
items.explanation: In high-prevalence countries, tuberculous osteomyelitis must routinely be considered mainly in very old people, HIV-infected patients, and immigrants from endemic countries.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY

items.subtopic: COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY
items.question: What is the most common cause of periprosthetic joint infection (PJI)?
items.explanation: About 50–70% of cases of PJI are caused by staphylococci (S. aureus and coagulase-negative staphylococci).
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY

items.question: Which of the following organisms are most commonly associated with chronic periprosthetic joint infection (PJI)?
items.explanation: Chronic PJI is most commonly caused by low-virulence microorganisms such as coagulase-negative staphylococci or C. acnes.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · CLASSIFICATION AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the threshold value of leukocytes/μL in synovial fluid for diagnosing periprosthetic knee infection?
items.explanation: The threshold value of leukocytes in synovial fluid for diagnosing periprosthetic knee infection is ~1700 leukocytes/μL.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · CLASSIFICATION AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which diagnostic test is highly specific but should not be used for screening due to its expense and limited sensitivity?
items.explanation: The synovial fluid biomarker α-defensin is highly specific but expensive and has limited sensitivity, making it unsuitable for screening.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · CLASSIFICATION AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: Periprosthetic Joint Infection | PREVENTION OF HEMATOGENOUS INFECTION
items.question: Which of the following conditions must be fulfilled for implant retention to offer a good chance of infection-free survival in periprosthetic joint infection?
options: (1) Acute infection, (2) Stable implant, (3) Pathogen susceptible to a biofilm-active antimicrobial agent, and (4) Skin and soft tissue in good condition
(1) Chronic infection, (2) Unstable implant, (3) Pathogen resistant to any antimicrobial agent, and (4) Poor skin and soft tissue condition
Only (1) Acute infection and (2) Stable implant
items.explanation: Implant retention offers a good chance of infection-free survival if all four conditions are met.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · Periprosthetic Joint Infection | PREVENTION OF HEMATOGENOUS INFECTION

items.subtopic: Periprosthetic Joint Infection | PREVENTION OF HEMATOGENOUS INFECTION
items.question: Which antibiotic should be combined with rifampin when treating staphylococcal implant-associated infections to prevent rapid emergence of resistance?
options: Another effective antibiotic must always be used in combination
Fluoroquinolones are the only antibiotics that should be combined with rifampin
Antibiotic therapy is not necessary if using rifampin
items.explanation: Rifampin must always be combined with another effective antibiotic to prevent rapid emergence of resistance.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · Periprosthetic Joint Infection | PREVENTION OF HEMATOGENOUS INFECTION

items.subtopic: Periprosthetic Joint Infection | PREVENTION OF HEMATOGENOUS INFECTION
items.question: What is the recommended approach for patients with prosthetic joints who have documented bacterial infections?
options: Promptly treat the infection to reduce risk of hematogenous seeding
Only treat if the infection is caused by S. aureus
Antibiotic prophylaxis during dental work is necessary
items.explanation: Documented bacterial infections should be promptly treated in patients with prosthetic joints to reduce risk of hematogenous seeding.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · Periprosthetic Joint Infection | PREVENTION OF HEMATOGENOUS INFECTION

items.question: Which antimicrobial agents are noted for their good activity against staphylococcal and gram-negative biofilms?
items.explanation: Rifampin is effective against staphylococcal biofilms, while fluoroquinolones are effective against gram-negative biofilms.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY

items.question: What is the primary cause of sternal osteomyelitis after surgery?
items.explanation: Sternal osteomyelitis occurs primarily due to the entry of exogenous organisms following sternal surgery.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY

items.question: Which microorganism is most commonly responsible for poststernotomy osteomyelitis?
items.explanation: Poststernotomy osteomyelitis is generally caused by S. aureus in 10–20% of cases.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY

items.question: Which condition is included in the differential diagnosis of hematogenous sternal osteomyelitis?
items.explanation: SAPHO syndrome is listed as a differential diagnosis for hematogenous sternal osteomyelitis.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Sternal Osteomyelitis

items.question: What imaging modality is considered the gold standard for detecting osteomyelitis?
items.explanation: MRI is stated as the current gold standard for detection of each type of osteomyelitis.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Sternal Osteomyelitis

items.question: In secondary sternal osteomyelitis, what is typically observed in leukocyte counts?
items.explanation: In secondary sternal osteomyelitis, leukocyte counts may be normal.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS | Sternal Osteomyelitis

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ■ PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | RISK FACTORS | MICROBIOLOGY
items.question: What is the mortality rate range for secondary sternal osteomyelitis during the first year after diagnosis?
items.explanation: The passage states that mortality rates from secondary sternal osteomyelitis during the first year after diagnosis are 6–20%.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ■ PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | RISK FACTORS | MICROBIOLOGY

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ■ PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | RISK FACTORS | MICROBIOLOGY
items.question: Which microorganisms should be considered in endemic areas during sampling for microbiologic diagnosis of osteomyelitis?
options: Escherichia coli, Klebsiella species, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa
Mycobacterium tuberculosis, Salmonella species, and Brucella species
Staphylococcus aureus, Enterococcus faecalis, and Streptococcus pyogenes
Candida albicans, Aspergillus fumigatus, and Cryptococcus neoformans
items.explanation: The passage mentions that in endemic areas, M. tuberculosis, Salmonella species, and Brucella species should be considered during sampling.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ■ PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | RISK FACTORS | MICROBIOLOGY

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ■ PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | RISK FACTORS | MICROBIOLOGY
items.question: Which pathogen has the highest correlation between needle biopsy and bone biopsy cultures in osteomyelitis?
items.explanation: The passage states that the correlation is better when S. aureus is isolated (40–50%) compared to other pathogens.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ■ PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | RISK FACTORS | MICROBIOLOGY

items.question: Which test has a positive predictive value of approximately 90% for diagnosing foot osteomyelitis in patients with a high pretest probability?
items.explanation: The 'probe-to-bone' test has a positive predictive value of ~90% in populations with a high pretest probability.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · DIAGNOSIS | Foot Osteomyelitis

items.question: In patients with a lower pretest probability for foot osteomyelitis, which imaging procedure should be performed due to its high sensitivity and specificity?
items.explanation: MRI should be performed in patients with a lower pretest probability because of its high sensitivity (80–100%) and specificity (80–90%).
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · DIAGNOSIS | Foot Osteomyelitis

items.question: What is the main utility of plain x-rays in the context of diabetic foot osteomyelitis?
items.explanation: Plain x-rays are mainly useful for follow-up of patients with confirmed diabetic foot osteomyelitis.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · DIAGNOSIS | Foot Osteomyelitis

items.statement: Acute hematogenous or contiguous osteomyelitis evolves over a period of weeks to months.
items.explanation: Acute hematogenous or contiguous osteomyelitis evolves over a short period—i.e., a few days or weeks.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · Osteomyelitis | CLASSIFICATION

items.statement: The Cierny-Mader staging system is useful for both systemic and local factors affecting immune status, metabolism, and local vascularity.
items.explanation: The Cierny-Mader staging system encompasses both systemic and local factors affecting immune status, metabolism, and local vascularity.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · Osteomyelitis | CLASSIFICATION

items.statement: Vertebral osteomyelitis is more common in female patients than male patients.
items.explanation: The passage states that vertebral osteomyelitis occurs more often in males than females, with a ratio of 1.5:1.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY

items.statement: In subacute/chronic vertebral osteomyelitis, the causative microorganisms are consistent worldwide.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that the microbiologic spectrum of subacute/chronic cases varies according to geographic region.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY

items.statement: Leukocytosis and neutrophilia have high diagnostic sensitivity for vertebral osteomyelitis.
items.explanation: Leukocytosis and neutrophilia have low levels of diagnostic sensitivity (only 65% and 40%, respectively).
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: MRI is the gold standard for diagnosing vertebral osteomyelitis.
items.explanation: MRI should be performed expeditiously in patients with neurologic impairment and is considered the gold standard.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Oral antibiotic therapy can be noninferior to intravenous therapy for vertebral osteomyelitis if certain conditions are met.
items.explanation: Oral antibiotics are noninferior to IV therapy under specific conditions such as optimal spectrum, bioavailability, and normal intestinal function.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · Vertebral Osteomyelitis

items.statement: Surgical treatment is always necessary in acute hematogenous vertebral osteomyelitis.
items.explanation: Surgical treatment is generally not needed in acute hematogenous vertebral osteomyelitis.

items.subtopic: COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | OSTEOMYELITIS IN LONG BONES | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.statement: The incidence rate of acute vertebral osteomyelitis varies significantly across different regions of the world.
items.explanation: The incidence rate of acute vertebral osteomyelitis is similar in different regions.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | OSTEOMYELITIS IN LONG BONES | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.subtopic: COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | OSTEOMYELITIS IN LONG BONES | EPIDEMIOLOGY
items.statement: Chronic osteomyelitis can be reactivated after a symptom-free interval of more than 70 years.
items.explanation: Chronic osteomyelitis can indeed be reactivated after such an extended period.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | OSTEOMYELITIS IN LONG BONES | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.statement: Fever and inflammatory markers are typical in Brodie's abscess.
items.explanation: Fever and inflammatory markers are indeed typical in Brodie's abscess.

items.statement: The median delay from symptoms to diagnosis of osteomyelitis is less than 1 month.
items.explanation: The median delay from symptoms to diagnosis of osteomyelitis is 3 months.

items.statement: Surgical exploration and sampling should be prompted by clinical and laboratory suspicion.
items.explanation: Clinical and laboratory suspicion should prompt surgical exploration and sampling.

items.statement: A three-phase bone scan is useful for diagnosing osteomyelitis immediately after surgery.
items.explanation: The three-phase bone scan is not useful during the interval of increased marker uptake from bone remodeling post-surgery.

items.subtopic: COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY
items.statement: The risk of infection is highest after hip and knee arthroplasty.
items.explanation: The risk of infection is lowest after hip and knee arthroplasty (0.3–1.5%).
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY

items.subtopic: COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY
items.statement: Hematogenous seeding of PJI occurs only in the early postoperative period.
items.explanation: Hematogenous seeding occurs throughout life, and most cases develop >2 years after implantation.

items.statement: Fever is a common symptom throughout all phases of periprosthetic joint infection.
items.explanation: Fever is rare after the initial phase of bacteremia in PJI.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · CLASSIFICATION AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: The three-phase bone scan can differentiate between bone remodeling and infection.
items.explanation: The three-phase bone scan is not specific and does not differentiate bone remodeling from infection.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · CLASSIFICATION AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Antibiotic prophylaxis is recommended during dental procedures for patients with prosthetic joints.
items.explanation: According to the passage, antibiotic prophylaxis is not needed during dental work.

items.subtopic: Periprosthetic Joint Infection | PREVENTION OF HEMATOGENOUS INFECTION
items.statement: The risk of prosthetic hip or knee infection increases following dental procedures.
items.explanation: A prospective case-control study showed that the risk is not increased following dental procedures.

items.statement: Primary sternal osteomyelitis accounts for the majority of all osteomyelitis cases.
items.explanation: Primary (hematogenous) sternal osteomyelitis accounts for only 0.3% of all cases of osteomyelitis.

items.statement: Polymicrobial cases are common in acute poststernotomy infections.
items.explanation: Polymicrobial cases are rare in acute infection and indicate exogenous superinfection during therapy.

items.statement: Superficial swab cultures are diagnostic for sternal osteomyelitis.
items.explanation: The passage states that superficial swab cultures are not diagnostic and may be misleading.

items.statement: In cases of deep sternal-wound infection, surgery is not required.
items.explanation: The passage specifies that a combined approach using both surgery and antibiotic treatment is required for deep sternal-wound infections.

items.subtopic: PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ■ PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | RISK FACTORS | MICROBIOLOGY
items.statement: Foot osteomyelitis is acquired almost exclusively by the endogenous route.
items.explanation: The passage specifies that foot osteomyelitis is acquired almost exclusively by the exogenous route.

items.statement: About 34% of people with diabetes develop a foot ulcer during their lifetime.
items.explanation: The passage states that about 34% of people with diabetes develop a foot ulcer during their lifetime.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · PROGNOSIS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | ■ PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | RISK FACTORS | MICROBIOLOGY

items.statement: Correlation between cultures of bone and those of wound swabs or punctures is strong.
items.explanation: The correlation between cultures of bone and those of wound swabs or punctures is poor.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · DIAGNOSIS | Foot Osteomyelitis

items.statement: Antibiotic treatment for foot osteomyelitis should be based on wound culture results.
items.explanation: Antibiotic treatment should be based on bone culture, not just wound cultures.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · DIAGNOSIS | Foot Osteomyelitis

items.assertion: Chronic osteomyelitis requires surgical intervention in addition to antibiotic treatment.
items.reason: Chronic osteomyelitis develops when insufficient therapy leads to persistence or recurrence, most often after sternal, mandibular, or foot infection.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · Osteomyelitis | CLASSIFICATION

items.assertion: The most common primary focus of infection in adults with hematogenous osteomyelitis is the endocardium.
items.reason: Hematogenous osteomyelitis in adults typically involves the vertebral column.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the most common primary foci include the urinary tract, skin/soft tissue, intravascular catheterization sites, and endocardium. The reason is true.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · Osteomyelitis | CLASSIFICATION

items.assertion: In vertebral osteomyelitis, fever >38°C occurs in over half of patients.
items.reason: Patients frequently use analgesic drugs which may mask the fever.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Endocarditis is identified in about 10% of patients with osteomyelitis.
items.reason: In cases caused by viridans streptococci, endocarditis is the source in half of the patients.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains why endocarditis can be a significant finding.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS | DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: Delayed or late infections in osteomyelitis are usually caused by high-virulence microorganisms.
items.reason: Delayed or late (chronic) infections are usually caused by low-virulence microorganisms or occur after ineffective treatment of early-onset infection.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because delayed or late infections are typically due to low-virulence microorganisms.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · MICROBIOLOGY | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY
items.assertion: Foreign devices are covered with host proteins such as fibronectin.
items.reason: This favors the adherence of staphylococci and formation of a biofilm that resists phagocytosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · COMPLICATIONS | GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY

items.subtopic: Periprosthetic Joint Infection | PREVENTION OF HEMATOGENOUS INFECTION
items.assertion: Antimicrobial therapy without surgical intervention can be curative for periprosthetic joint infection.
items.reason: As a rule, antimicrobial therapy without surgical intervention is not curative but merely suppressive.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because antimicrobial therapy alone is not curative.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · Periprosthetic Joint Infection | PREVENTION OF HEMATOGENOUS INFECTION

items.assertion: Rifampin resistance in staphylococci affects the cure rate of debridement with implant retention.
items.reason: In countries with high rates of rifampin resistance, this treatment generally does not yield a good cure rate.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Osteomyelitis · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS | PATHOGENESIS | EPIDEMIOLOGY | MICROBIOLOGY
